title: Ectopic Pregnancy
oneLiner: A pregnancy implanted outside the uterine cavity, typically in the fallopian tube; can rupture causing life-threatening haemorrhage in young women.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ruptured ectopic with haemorrhagic shock** — the single finding that says "this is the killer, not a routine presentation": **tachycardia with postural hypotension or syncope** in a woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain. Bedside ultrasound (if available) showing free fluid in the pouch of Douglas is a red flag.
- **Abdominal compartment syndrome from massive intra-abdominal bleeding** — look for **distended, rigid abdomen with absent bowel sounds and falling urine output** despite adequate IV fluids. This is a surgical emergency requiring immediate laparotomy.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Abdominal pain (often unilateral, lower quadrant, may be acute or chronic)
  - Vaginal bleeding (light, dark, often described as "spotting")
  - Amenorrhoea (variable – up to 10% of ectopics have no history of missed period)
  - Shoulder tip pain or syncope (suggests diaphragmatic irritation from intra-abdominal blood)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cervical excitation (positive "chandelier sign")
  - Adnexal tenderness and/or mass
  - Haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) if ruptured
  - Abdominal distension/guarding if haemoperitoneum present
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Miscarriage (threatened or incomplete):** vaginal bleeding + pain, but transvaginal ultrasound shows intrauterine gestational sac (when ≥5.5 weeks)
- **Ovarian cyst accident (rupture, torsion or haemorrhage):** acute pain, but absence of amenorrhoea or positive pregnancy test differentiates; ultrasound shows cyst rather than empty uterus+adnexal mass
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID):** bilateral pain, fever, cervical motion tenderness, but pregnancy test negative
- **Appendicitis:** pain migrates to right iliac fossa, anorexia, fever; pregnancy test negative, ultrasound shows no adnexal mass
- **Corpus luteum cyst:** mild pain, no bleeding, ultrasound shows simple cyst; pregnancy test positive only if early intrauterine pregnancy (expectant management)
- **Splitter:** The single most discriminating bedside test is **urine pregnancy test** — if negative, ectopic is effectively excluded (sensitivity >99%) and you should redirect to non-pregnancy causes (e.g. ovarian torsion, PID)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine or serum β-hCG (immediate if not already done)
  - Vital signs including postural BP
  - Abdominal and pelvic exam (gentle)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Quantitative β-hCG** (immediately, then serial if unclear)
  - FBC, U+Es, LFTs, coagulation screen
  - Group & save (crossmatch if unstable or high risk)
  - RhD typing and antibody screen (unless known Rh positive)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) — the gold standard. Key findings: empty uterus with a "tubal ring" or adnexal mass, free fluid in pelvis. If no IUP and β-hCG >1500 IU/L (discriminatory zone) → high likelihood of ectopic.
  - **Second-line:** If TVUS inconclusive and β-hCG <1500 IU/L with stable patient → serial β-hCG at 48h intervals. A rise <53% over 48h is suggestive of ectopic. Consider diagnostic laparoscopy if still uncertain.
- **Special tests:** Rhesus D status (all Rh-negative women need anti-D immunoglobulin; dose 250 IU for <12 weeks, 500 IU for >12 weeks — [check local policy] for exact dose)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** (urine or serum) in any woman of reproductive age (12–55 years) presenting with any abdominal pain, syncope, or vaginal bleeding. Omission is a serious error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If unstable (hypotensive, tachycardia, NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2):** urgent senior gynaecology review, IV access (two large-bore cannulae), crossmatch 4 units, start IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s 500 mL bolus, repeated as needed). Do not delay surgery for imaging — proceed to laparotomy/laparoscopy.
  - **If stable:** confirm β-hCG, arrange TVUS. Book early pregnancy unit (EPU) review.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Surgical:** Laparoscopic salpingectomy or salpingotomy (if future fertility desired and contralateral tube healthy). Salpingectomy is standard.
  - **Medical (methotrexate):** for unruptured ectopic with β-hCG <1500 IU/L, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <35 mm, and reliable patient. Give methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM single dose (use body surface area). Check β-hCG on day 4 and day 7; aim for ≥15% drop from baseline. If not, consider second dose.
  - **Expectant management:** if β-hCG <1500 IU/L and falling spontaneously – monitor with serial β-hCG until negative. Usually inpatient for first 48h.
  - **Analgesia:** paracetamol 1g QDS, NSAIDs if no contraindication (but avoid if high risk of bleeding or renal impairment).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow-up in EPU until β-hCG <5 IU/L (if medical/expectant).
  - Advise to avoid sexual intercourse until β-hCG negative.
  - Arrange anti-D immunoglobulin if Rh negative (dose as per local policy).
  - Counsel about recurrence risk (~10%) and future fertility.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV Hartmann’s at 80 mL/h if NBM; otherwise oral if haemodynamically stable
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (but caution if high bleeding risk from surgery – check local protocol)
  - Diet: starve promptly if surgical option is likely (NBM from time of diagnosis)
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable, 1-hourly if any abnormality
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Methotrexate (first-line medical):**
  - Dose: 50 mg/m² IM single dose (calculate BSA using Mosteller formula or chart)
  - Duration: single dose, then β-hCG monitoring on day 4 and 7
  - **Common alternatives:** second dose may be required if suboptimal day 4–7 drop (<15%)
- **Anti-D immunoglobulin (for Rh-negative women):**
  - Dose: 250 IU IM (for <12 weeks gestation) or 500 IU IM (>12 weeks) — [check local policy]
  - Timing: ideally within 72h of diagnosis/surgery
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS
  - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (if no contraindication)
  - Morphine IV 1–2 mg boluses PRN for severe pain (with antiemetic)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Methotrexate: avoid in immunosuppression, significant renal impairment (eGFR <30), active infection, pregnancy (must confirm no IUP), liver disease. Check LFTs and eGFR before use.
  - NSAIDs: avoid if active bleeding, coagulopathy, renal impairment, or history of peptic ulcer
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Methotrexate: reduce dose or avoid if eGFR <30. In mild impairment, consider [check local protocol]. Maximum single dose is rarely adjusted but require monitoring.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Methotrexate — giving it to a patient with unrecognised ruptured ectopic or with an intrauterine pregnancy (i.e. no confirmatory scan)**. Always confirm no IUP on TVUS before methotrexate. Also: failure to check β-hCG trend before giving second dose can lead to toxicity.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhage (rupture) → hypovolaemic shock, death
  - Tubal rupture → can occur even with low β-hCG (<1000 IU/L)
  - Methotrexate side effects (nausea, stomatitis, bone marrow suppression)
- **Chronic:**
  - Infertility (tubal damage, especially if salpingectomy)
  - Recurrent ectopic pregnancy (~10-15% risk in later pregnancies)
  - Persistent ectopic pregnancy (failed methotrexate/salpingotomy → need second treatment)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality ~0.2/1000 ectopic pregnancies in UK (RCOG data). With early diagnosis and treatment, recovery is full; future fertility depends on contralateral tubal health. Expectant management success rates ~30-50%; methotrexate success ~70-80%; surgical salvage >95%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check a pregnancy test** in any woman aged 12–55 with abdominal pain or syncope – it is the single most important action.
- **Use the β-hCG discriminatory zone with TVUS** – if no IUP seen and β-hCG >1500 IU/L, the likelihood of ectopic is high; escalate to senior clinician.
- **Give anti-D immunoglobulin** as soon as possible to all Rh-negative women – do not delay for β-hCG result.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on β-hCG alone to rule out ectopic** – a low β-hCG does not exclude rupture; clinical stability is paramount.
- **Do not give methotrexate without a confirmatory TVUS** showing no IUP and no adnexal mass with fetal cardiac activity.
- **Do not discharge a patient with possible ectopic** without a clear plan for serial β-hCG and a named contact in the early pregnancy unit.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Severe worsening abdominal pain, heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking more than 1 pad per hour), feeling faint or fainting, shoulder tip pain, or fever.
- **Follow-up:** Early pregnancy unit within 24–48h for serial β-hCG check; if methotrexate given, schedule day 4 and day 7 β-hCG. Advise to avoid alcohol until treatment complete.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call gynaecology registrar or consultant immediately for:**
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >120, NEWS2 ≥5)
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 (especially after syncope)
  - Any suspicion of rupture (shoulder tip pain, hypotension, rigid abdomen)
- **If unstable and gynaecologist not immediately available, call crash team and prepare for emergency laparotomy** with general surgery support. Do not wait for imaging.

rawMarkdown:
## Ectopic Pregnancy
**One-liner:** A pregnancy implanted outside the uterine cavity, typically in the fallopian tube; can rupture causing life-threatening haemorrhage in young women.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ruptured ectopic with haemorrhagic shock** — the single finding that says "this is the killer, not a routine presentation": **tachycardia with postural hypotension or syncope** in a woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain. Bedside ultrasound (if available) showing free fluid in the pouch of Douglas is a red flag.
- **Abdominal compartment syndrome from massive intra-abdominal bleeding** — look for **distended, rigid abdomen with absent bowel sounds and falling urine output** despite adequate IV fluids. This is a surgical emergency requiring immediate laparotomy.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Abdominal pain (often unilateral, lower quadrant, may be acute or chronic)
  - Vaginal bleeding (light, dark, often described as "spotting")
  - Amenorrhoea (variable – up to 10% of ectopics have no history of missed period)
  - Shoulder tip pain or syncope (suggests diaphragmatic irritation from intra-abdominal blood)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cervical excitation (positive "chandelier sign")
  - Adnexal tenderness and/or mass
  - Haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) if ruptured
  - Abdominal distension/guarding if haemoperitoneum present

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Miscarriage (threatened or incomplete):** vaginal bleeding + pain, but transvaginal ultrasound shows intrauterine gestational sac (when ≥5.5 weeks)
- **Ovarian cyst accident (rupture, torsion or haemorrhage):** acute pain, but absence of amenorrhoea or positive pregnancy test differentiates; ultrasound shows cyst rather than empty uterus+adnexal mass
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID):** bilateral pain, fever, cervical motion tenderness, but pregnancy test negative
- **Appendicitis:** pain migrates to right iliac fossa, anorexia, fever; pregnancy test negative, ultrasound shows no adnexal mass
- **Corpus luteum cyst:** mild pain, no bleeding, ultrasound shows simple cyst; pregnancy test positive only if early intrauterine pregnancy (expectant management)
- **Splitter:** The single most discriminating bedside test is **urine pregnancy test** — if negative, ectopic is effectively excluded (sensitivity >99%) and you should redirect to non-pregnancy causes (e.g. ovarian torsion, PID)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine or serum β-hCG (immediate if not already done)
  - Vital signs including postural BP
  - Abdominal and pelvic exam (gentle)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Quantitative β-hCG** (immediately, then serial if unclear)
  - FBC, U+Es, LFTs, coagulation screen
  - Group & save (crossmatch if unstable or high risk)
  - RhD typing and antibody screen (unless known Rh positive)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) — the gold standard. Key findings: empty uterus with a "tubal ring" or adnexal mass, free fluid in pelvis. If no IUP and β-hCG >1500 IU/L (discriminatory zone) → high likelihood of ectopic.
  - **Second-line:** If TVUS inconclusive and β-hCG <1500 IU/L with stable patient → serial β-hCG at 48h intervals. A rise <53% over 48h is suggestive of ectopic. Consider diagnostic laparoscopy if still uncertain.
- **Special tests:** Rhesus D status (all Rh-negative women need anti-D immunoglobulin; dose 250 IU for <12 weeks, 500 IU for >12 weeks — [check local policy] for exact dose)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** (urine or serum) in any woman of reproductive age (12–55 years) presenting with any abdominal pain, syncope, or vaginal bleeding. Omission is a serious error.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If unstable (hypotensive, tachycardia, NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2):** urgent senior gynaecology review, IV access (two large-bore cannulae), crossmatch 4 units, start IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s 500 mL bolus, repeated as needed). Do not delay surgery for imaging — proceed to laparotomy/laparoscopy.
  - **If stable:** confirm β-hCG, arrange TVUS. Book early pregnancy unit (EPU) review.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Surgical:** Laparoscopic salpingectomy or salpingotomy (if future fertility desired and contralateral tube healthy). Salpingectomy is standard.
  - **Medical (methotrexate):** for unruptured ectopic with β-hCG <1500 IU/L, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <35 mm, and reliable patient. Give methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM single dose (use body surface area). Check β-hCG on day 4 and day 7; aim for ≥15% drop from baseline. If not, consider second dose.
  - **Expectant management:** if β-hCG <1500 IU/L and falling spontaneously – monitor with serial β-hCG until negative. Usually inpatient for first 48h.
  - **Analgesia:** paracetamol 1g QDS, NSAIDs if no contraindication (but avoid if high risk of bleeding or renal impairment).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow-up in EPU until β-hCG <5 IU/L (if medical/expectant).
  - Advise to avoid sexual intercourse until β-hCG negative.
  - Arrange anti-D immunoglobulin if Rh negative (dose as per local policy).
  - Counsel about recurrence risk (~10%) and future fertility.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV Hartmann’s at 80 mL/h if NBM; otherwise oral if haemodynamically stable
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (but caution if high bleeding risk from surgery – check local protocol)
  - Diet: starve promptly if surgical option is likely (NBM from time of diagnosis)
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable, 1-hourly if any abnormality

### Drugs & Doses
- **Methotrexate (first-line medical):**
  - Dose: 50 mg/m² IM single dose (calculate BSA using Mosteller formula or chart)
  - Duration: single dose, then β-hCG monitoring on day 4 and 7
  - **Common alternatives:** second dose may be required if suboptimal day 4–7 drop (<15%)
- **Anti-D immunoglobulin (for Rh-negative women):**
  - Dose: 250 IU IM (for <12 weeks gestation) or 500 IU IM (>12 weeks) — [check local policy]
  - Timing: ideally within 72h of diagnosis/surgery
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS
  - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (if no contraindication)
  - Morphine IV 1–2 mg boluses PRN for severe pain (with antiemetic)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Methotrexate: avoid in immunosuppression, significant renal impairment (eGFR <30), active infection, pregnancy (must confirm no IUP), liver disease. Check LFTs and eGFR before use.
  - NSAIDs: avoid if active bleeding, coagulopathy, renal impairment, or history of peptic ulcer
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Methotrexate: reduce dose or avoid if eGFR <30. In mild impairment, consider [check local protocol]. Maximum single dose is rarely adjusted but require monitoring.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Methotrexate — giving it to a patient with unrecognised ruptured ectopic or with an intrauterine pregnancy (i.e. no confirmatory scan)**. Always confirm no IUP on TVUS before methotrexate. Also: failure to check β-hCG trend before giving second dose can lead to toxicity.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhage (rupture) → hypovolaemic shock, death
  - Tubal rupture → can occur even with low β-hCG (<1000 IU/L)
  - Methotrexate side effects (nausea, stomatitis, bone marrow suppression)
- **Chronic:**
  - Infertility (tubal damage, especially if salpingectomy)
  - Recurrent ectopic pregnancy (~10-15% risk in later pregnancies)
  - Persistent ectopic pregnancy (failed methotrexate/salpingotomy → need second treatment)

### Prognosis
- Mortality ~0.2/1000 ectopic pregnancies in UK (RCOG data). With early diagnosis and treatment, recovery is full; future fertility depends on contralateral tubal health. Expectant management success rates ~30-50%; methotrexate success ~70-80%; surgical salvage >95%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check a pregnancy test** in any woman aged 12–55 with abdominal pain or syncope – it is the single most important action.
- **Use the β-hCG discriminatory zone with TVUS** – if no IUP seen and β-hCG >1500 IU/L, the likelihood of ectopic is high; escalate to senior clinician.
- **Give anti-D immunoglobulin** as soon as possible to all Rh-negative women – do not delay for β-hCG result.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on β-hCG alone to rule out ectopic** – a low β-hCG does not exclude rupture; clinical stability is paramount.
- **Do not give methotrexate without a confirmatory TVUS** showing no IUP and no adnexal mass with fetal cardiac activity.
- **Do not discharge a patient with possible ectopic** without a clear plan for serial β-hCG and a named contact in the early pregnancy unit.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Severe worsening abdominal pain, heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking more than 1 pad per hour), feeling faint or fainting, shoulder tip pain, or fever.
- **Follow-up:** Early pregnancy unit within 24–48h for serial β-hCG check; if methotrexate given, schedule day 4 and day 7 β-hCG. Advise to avoid alcohol until treatment complete.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call gynaecology registrar or consultant immediately for:**
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >120, NEWS2 ≥5)
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 (especially after syncope)
  - Any suspicion of rupture (shoulder tip pain, hypotension, rigid abdomen)
- **If unstable and gynaecologist not immediately available, call crash team and prepare for emergency laparotomy** with general surgery support. Do not wait for imaging.
